items.text: Chapter Six
Morphological Description of Tuta absoluta
Part One: General Introduction to Morphological Description
Introduction

Morphological description refers to the study of the external and structural characteristics that distinguish a living organism, such as shape, size, color, body segmentation, appendages, wings, setae, and diagnostic markings.

Morphological description provides an essential basis for identifying Tuta absoluta and distinguishing it from other pests that attack tomato crops, particularly species that may cause similar symptoms, such as leaf-mining flies, potato tuber moth, tomato pinworm, and tomato fruitworm.
Tuta absoluta undergoes complete metamorphosis involving four clearly distinct stages that differ in both form and function:
The transition between these stages is not merely a gradual increase in size. Each stage differs from the others in body structure, behavior, location on the plant, and role within the life cycle.

The egg represents the beginning of a new individual, the larva is the feeding and damaging stage, the pupa is the stage of body reorganization and transformation, while the adult moth represents the reproductive, dispersal, and egg-laying stage.
Importance of Morphological Description in Pest Diagnosis
Finding a mine inside a tomato leaf is not sufficient to confirm the presence of Tuta absoluta, because several insects are capable of forming mines within leaves.
Likewise, the presence of a larva inside a tomato fruit is not sufficient to conclude that it is a Tuta larva, because fruits may be infested by other larval species.
Correct diagnosis depends on combining several lines of evidence, including:

items: Presence of a distinct head and legs.
Shape of the mine and the frass present within it.
Location of the pupa and nature of the surrounding covering.

items.text: The level of diagnostic confidence increases as several compatible characteristics are observed together.
Complete Metamorphosis in Tuta absoluta
Tuta absoluta belongs to the insects that undergo complete metamorphosis, a type of development known as:
Complete Metamorphosis or Holometabolous Development
In this type of development, the larva differs clearly from the adult insect.
The larva:

items: Moves within mines and between plant parts.
Represents the stage responsible for agricultural damage.

items.text: The adult insect:

items: Does not produce mines or holes within plant tissues.
Transfers infestation between plants and fields.

items.text: The pupal stage separates these two stages. During pupation, profound internal and external changes occur, leading to the formation of the adult moth.
Morphological Changes During the Life Cycle

The insect begins as a very small egg, usually laid singly on plant parts. After embryonic development is completed, a minute larva emerges and begins searching for a suitable location to penetrate plant tissue.
The larva passes through four larval instars:
Each instar is separated from the next by a molt, during which the larva sheds its old external covering so that the new covering can accommodate an increase in body size.
The larva does not change only in size; changes also occur in:

items: Ability of the larva to move between plant parts.

items.text: After completing the fourth larval instar, the larva stops feeding, enters the prepupal stage, and then transforms into a pupa.
After metamorphosis is completed, a small moth emerges from the pupa. The moth differs completely from the larva in body form, wings, mouthparts, and biological function.
Morphological and Biological Characteristics
Morphological description must be distinguished from biological description.
Morphological Description
It addresses:
Biological Description
It addresses:
The two fields may be related, but egg length or larval color is a morphological characteristic, whereas egg-incubation period or larval growth rate is a biological characteristic.
Variation in Color and Size
The color of the different stages of Tuta absoluta is not completely constant, but changes according to several factors, most importantly:

items: Proximity to hatching or moth emergence.

items.text: A newly laid egg differs in color from an egg approaching hatching, a newly hatched larva differs from a fully grown larva, and a newly formed pupa differs from one approaching adult emergence.
For this reason, color alone should not be used for diagnosis. It should be interpreted together with size, shape, location of the specimen, and other anatomical characteristics.
Published measurements also vary to a limited extent among studies as a result of differences in:

items: Whether the specimen is alive or preserved.
Exact age within the developmental stage.

items.text: When measurements are presented, an approximate range should therefore be used to represent natural variation rather than treating a single number as a fixed limit for all individuals.
Field Examination and Laboratory Examination
Many characteristics of Tuta absoluta can be provisionally identified in the field using:

items: Mobile-phone camera equipped with a magnifying lens.

items.text: However, some fine characteristics cannot be confirmed with the naked eye, particularly:
These characteristics require a stereomicroscope, and final confirmation in difficult cases may require molecular analysis.
Collecting Specimens Without Damaging Them
Different life stages require appropriate collection methods.
Eggs
A small section of the leaf surrounding the egg should be cut rather than attempting to remove the egg directly, because slight pressure may crush it.
Larvae
The mine should be opened gradually using a needle or fine forceps while avoiding pressure on the larval body.
Pupae
Pupae should be collected together with part of the leaf, cocoon, or surrounding soil, because forceful removal of the covering may damage the pupa.
Adults
Adults should be collected from traps or plants while preserving the wings and scales, because excessive friction causes scales to detach and alters the external appearance.
Specimen information should be recorded, including:

items: Type of trap when the moth is collected from a trap.

items.text: Difference Between Field Diagnosis and Taxonomic Confirmation
An agricultural engineer can diagnose infestation in the field with a good degree of confidence when several characteristic signs occur together, such as:

items: Presence of frass inside the mine.
A larva with a distinct head and legs.
Presence of the dark band behind the head in advanced larvae.
Infestation of growing points and fruits.
Capture of Tuta moths in pheromone traps.

items.text: However, taxonomic confirmation becomes necessary when:

items: The pest appears in an area where it has not previously been recorded.
Results will be used in scientific publication.
A plant-quarantine study is being conducted.
The specimen is damaged or incomplete.
Trap results and field symptoms are inconsistent.

items.text: In such cases, diagnosis depends on microscopic or molecular characteristics rather than on the appearance of damage alone.
Relationship Between Morphology and the Location of Each Stage
The location where a life stage is found provides supporting evidence, but is not sufficient by itself.

Eggs may be found on leaves or young plant parts, whereas larvae occur inside leaves, stems, or fruits. Pupae may form inside a folded leaf, within a silken cocoon, or near the soil surface. Adult moths commonly hide among plants, and males can be monitored using pheromone traps.
However, these locations may vary according to conditions. Diagnosis should therefore combine:
Diagnostic Sequence of the Life Stages
Identification of the insect proceeds from the smallest stage to the largest stage:
Egg
Its shape, color, size, oviposition site, and changes occurring before hatching are examined.
Larva
The larval instar, color, head capsule, legs, prothoracic shield, body form, and distinguishing characteristics from other leaf-mining insects are examined.
Pupa
Its shape, color, length, location, presence of a silken cocoon, and changes preceding moth emergence are examined.
Adult Insect
Body length, wingspan, body and wing color, dark markings, fringes, antennae, and sexual differences are examined.
These stages are presented in detail in the subsequent parts of this chapter.
Part Two: The Egg
Morphological Characteristics, Incubation, Hatching, and Applied Importance
Introduction

The egg represents the first stage in the life cycle of Tuta absoluta, from which the formation of a new generation begins within the field. Although the egg does not feed on the plant and causes no direct damage, it represents a critical stage in the development of infestation because every viable, unparasitized egg can develop into a mining larva that penetrates plant tissues within a short period after hatching.

The egg stage is particularly important in integrated pest management because it is the final stage before the larva enters leaves, growing points, stems, or fruits. Once the larva penetrates plant tissue, it becomes more protected from contact insecticides and from many environmental factors and natural enemies.
The Tuta egg is characterized by the following:

items: Attached directly to the plant surface.
Its color is close to that of plant tissues.
Its incubation period is affected by temperature.
It may be exposed to predation, parasitism, desiccation, and non-viability.
Its rate of development increases as temperature rises within the suitable range.
It hatches within a few days under warm conditions.

items.text: Therefore, egg monitoring is not merely a description of the insect’s life cycle; it is also an early-warning tool for predicting the emergence of newly hatched larvae.
Egg Shape
The egg of Tuta absoluta is small, oval or elliptical in shape, and may appear short-cylindrical when viewed from the side.
It is characterized by the following:

items: Its length is greater than its width.
Both ends are rounded rather than pointed.
It adheres directly to plant tissue.
It is not borne on a long stalk.
It is not usually laid in large, organized masses.
Its longitudinal axis is approximately parallel to the surface of the leaf or stem.

items.text: Describing the egg as oval does not conflict with describing it as short-cylindrical, because its apparent shape varies according to the viewing angle. It appears oval when viewed from above, while the body appears elongated and slightly raised when viewed from the side.
Egg Measurements
The usual measurements of a Tuta absoluta egg are approximately:
The average measurement reported in several studies is approximately:
0.38 mm long × 0.21 mm wide.
These measurements mean that the egg is far too small to be examined efficiently with the naked eye. Detection requires:

items: Concentration on young leaves and plant growing points.
A stereomicroscope when confirmation of viability or parasitism is required.

items.text: Measurements may vary slightly as a result of differences in:

items: Position of the egg during examination.
Whether the specimen is alive or preserved.

items.text: Egg Color and Its Change During Development
Egg color does not remain constant from oviposition until hatching; rather, it changes gradually as the embryo develops.
Newly Laid Egg
A newly laid egg is usually:
This coloration makes the egg difficult to detect on tomato leaves, especially in the presence of plant trichomes, dust, and residues of spray solutions.
Mid-Incubation Period
As embryonic development continues, the color changes to:

items: Sometimes yellow with a light orange tinge.

items.text: The embryo gradually becomes visible through the semi-transparent eggshell.
Egg Approaching Hatching
Before hatching, the egg becomes darker, and a dark area becomes visible inside it, representing the larval head and head capsule.
This stage is known as:
Blackhead Stage
At this stage, hatching is imminent, especially at high temperatures.

The appearance of a small dark area inside the egg does not mean that the egg has been parasitized. In a healthy egg approaching hatching, the larval head appears on one side, whereas parasitized eggs tend to darken more uniformly.
Eggshell
The egg is surrounded by an outer shell known as the:
Chorion
This shell performs several functions:

items: Protecting the embryo from mechanical damage.
Partially isolating the embryo from some external influences.

items.text: The presence of the chorion does not mean that the egg is completely protected, because it can still be affected by heat, desiccation, insecticides with ovicidal activity, parasitoids, and predators.
Penetration of control products through the shell varies according to:

items: Presence of a surfactant or oil.
Position of the egg on the plant.
Presence of trichomes or wax layers on the leaf.

items.text: Oviposition Pattern
The female Tuta absoluta usually lays eggs singly, although two or several eggs may occasionally be found close together in a small, irregular group.
The female does not usually form large egg masses as occurs in some other insects.
Laying eggs singly provides the insect with several advantages:

items: Distributing offspring over several parts of the plant.
Reducing competition among newly hatched larvae.
Reducing the probability of losing all eggs at once.
Making eggs more difficult to detect and remove.
Increasing the probability that larvae will reach suitable mining sites.
Reducing exposure of all eggs to a single predator or parasitoid.

items.text: The female may lay eggs on several plants within the field, helping to spread the infestation rather than concentrating it on a single plant.
Distribution of Eggs on Tomato Plant Parts
The female does not distribute eggs equally among all plant parts, but prefers young leaves and tissues near the plant apex.
The commonly reported approximate distribution of eggs is as follows:
These percentages represent a common pattern reported in the literature and are not fixed values for every field, cultivar, or crop-growth stage.
Egg distribution is affected by:

items: Location at which moths enter the field.

items.text: Distribution on Leaves
Young leaves represent the principal oviposition sites, especially recently fully expanded leaves located in the upper part of the plant.
Females prefer these leaves because they:

items: Are close to the growing point.
Provide tender tissues for newly hatched larvae.
Permit easier penetration of leaf tissue.
Are close to growing points, flowers, and young fruits.

items.text: Before flowering, oviposition occurs more frequently on the lower leaf surface, whereas females may use both upper and lower surfaces to a greater extent after the plant develops and enters flowering and fruiting.
Vertical Distribution on the Plant
Most eggs are concentrated in the upper or younger layer of the canopy at the time of oviposition.

However, the position of the leaf changes as the plant grows. An egg may be laid near the apex, but after several days the leaf may become positioned lower because the stem continues elongating and new leaves are produced above it.
Inspection should therefore not be limited to the uppermost two leaves, but should include:

items: Both upper and lower surfaces of leaflets.

items.text: Distribution Within the Field
Eggs are not distributed completely at random within the field, but tend to have an aggregated distribution.
At the beginning of invasion, their density increases in:

items: The side facing an old infested field.
Areas near infested nurseries or greenhouses.
The direction from which moth-carrying winds enter.
The first plants in planting rows.

items.text: Open-field studies have shown that egg aggregations are often greater at field edges and that spread along planting rows may occur faster than movement across rows.
Egg Incubation Period
The egg incubation period is the time from oviposition on the plant until larval emergence.
The egg does not have a fixed incubation period because the rate of embryonic development depends primarily on temperature.
Under moderate and warm conditions, eggs usually hatch within:
4–6 days.
However, this period may be shortened to approximately two or three days under suitably high temperatures, or extended to more than ten days under low temperatures.
Effect of Temperature on Incubation Period
Embryonic growth becomes faster as temperature increases within the suitable range. Therefore, temperature and incubation period are inversely related:
> The higher the suitable temperature, the shorter the incubation period; the lower the temperature, the longer the period.
One controlled-temperature study reported the following results:

rows: No hatching occurred in this study

items.text: The data show that increasing temperature from 15 to 25°C reduced the incubation period from approximately 12 days to approximately 4.5 days.
Another study conducted at a relative humidity of 60±10% reported the following results:
Differences between the two studies demonstrate that the response of eggs to extreme heat is not identical among all populations and experimental conditions.
Results differ because of:

items: Adaptation of insects to local climate.
Differences in host plant or cultivar.
Differences in egg age at the time of temperature exposure.
Constant laboratory temperature versus fluctuating field temperature.
Differences in the definition of egg viability, survival, or hatching percentage.

items.text: Egg-Hatching Percentages at Different Temperatures
In an experiment conducted at a relative humidity of 60±10%, the following hatching percentages were recorded:
However, egg-hatching percentage alone is not sufficient to determine the optimum temperature for the pest.
Although high hatching was recorded at 30°C in this experiment, survival from egg to adult declined to approximately 33.33%, compared with approximately 76.67% at 25°C.
At 35°C, 70% of eggs hatched, but the percentage of individuals completing development from egg to adult did not exceed 10%.
This demonstrates that rapid hatching or a high hatching percentage in a particular experiment does not mean that the same temperature is optimal for completion of the entire generation.
Differences Among Studies at High Temperatures
Another thermal study recorded egg survival or hatching percentages as follows:
No eggs hatched at 35°C in this study.
This differs clearly from the study that recorded 100% hatching at 30°C and 70% at 35°C.

This scientific difference is important because it shows that temperatures close to the upper threshold produce highly variable results, and a single percentage cannot be considered a fixed rule for all pest populations.
The most consistent conclusion across studies is that:

items: Severe cold slows development and reduces hatching.
A temperature of 25°C repeatedly provides a short incubation period and high hatching.
Temperatures of 30–35°C accelerate development, but survival may become unstable.
Prolonged high temperature adversely affects the subsequent stages even when some eggs hatch successfully.

items.text: Most Suitable Temperature for Egg Incubation and Hatching
The most suitable and stable thermal range for egg development is:
A temperature of approximately:
Is considered one of the best practical temperatures for egg incubation because it combines:

items: A short incubation period of approximately 4–5 days.
High survival of larvae and pupae after hatching.
A high percentage of generation completion.

items.text: A temperature of 30°C may result in faster hatching within approximately 2.5–4 days, but it does not always provide the highest survival, and results differ greatly among populations and studies.
The range of 20–25°C is also among the most suitable ranges for the overall development, survival, and reproduction of the pest, while the optimum estimated by some models lies between 21 and 23°C.
Thermal Thresholds for Egg Development
One study estimated the lower developmental threshold for eggs at approximately:
The thermal constant required to complete the egg stage was estimated at approximately:
58.5 accumulated heat units — Degree-Days
The upper thermal threshold for egg development was estimated at approximately:
These values mean that embryonic development is extremely slow near 10°C and stops or becomes abnormal at low or high temperatures exceeding physiological limits.
In another experiment:

items: Only approximately 17% of eggs hatched at 10°C.
Individuals hatching at 10°C were unable to complete development to the adult stage.

items.text: Approximate Calculation of Hatching Time Using Degree-Days
Daily heat accumulation can be calculated in a simplified form using the following relationship:
Daily degree-days = Mean daily temperature − 9.6
A negative value is not counted when the mean is below the lower threshold.
Hatching is expected to approach when approximately:
58.5 degree-days
Have accumulated.
Example at a mean daily temperature of 25°C:
This estimate is close to the laboratory incubation period recorded at 25°C, which ranged around 4.4–4.5 days.
This calculation remains approximate because leaf-surface temperature may differ from air temperature, and temperatures fluctuate between day and night.
Effect of Relative Humidity on Eggs
Relative humidity affects water balance within the egg and the embryo’s ability to complete development.
A severe decrease in humidity, particularly when combined with high temperature, increases:
In a study comparing two thermal and humidity regimes, egg survival reached:
This comparison represents the combined effect of temperature and humidity and does not separate the effect of humidity alone because temperature increased at the same time that humidity declined.
Moderate humidity, which in many rearing experiments lies around 55–70%, provides favorable conditions for egg development, provided that temperature remains within the suitable range.
Very high humidity accompanied by continuous wetness or water condensation may lead to:

items: Increased fungal growth on the leaf surface.
Reduced gas exchange when the egg is covered with water.
Changes in the efficacy of some control products.

items.text: Effect of Extreme Heat
Eggs may tolerate short periods of heat better than continuous exposure in some cases, but severe heat stress reduces hatching.
Heat-stress experiments showed that exposing eggs to:
44°C for six hours
Significantly reduced hatching percentage.
The degree of damage varies according to:

items: Whether the egg is exposed to direct sunlight or shade.

items.text: Leaf or fruit surface temperature may exceed measured air temperature, especially under direct solar radiation, thereby increasing egg exposure to desiccation and heat stress.
Egg Sensitivity to Different Factors
Sensitivity to Cold
Cold conditions lead to:
At 10°C, the incubation period may extend beyond 20 days, whereas hatching may stop at temperatures close to 7°C in some experiments.
Delayed hatching under cold conditions does not necessarily result in the death of all eggs. Some may resume development when temperature rises, depending on the duration and intensity of exposure.
Sensitivity to High Temperature
High temperatures within the acceptable range shorten the incubation period, but prolonged exposure near the upper threshold causes:
Conflicting results at 30–35°C demonstrate that the capacity of eggs to hatch does not guarantee normal completion of subsequent development.
Sensitivity to Desiccation
Eggs exposed on the plant surface are subject to water loss, particularly when the following occur together:
The outer shell reduces water loss but does not prevent it completely.
Newly laid eggs or eggs damaged mechanically are more susceptible to desiccation than intact eggs with a complete shell.
Sensitivity to Rainfall and Overhead Irrigation
Heavy rain or overhead-irrigation droplets may mechanically affect some exposed eggs through:

items: Dislodging them from the plant surface.
Altering the temperature and humidity surrounding them.

items.text: However, adhesion of the egg to plant tissue makes water washing an unreliable control method, and rainfall or overhead irrigation does not remove all eggs.
Sensitivity to Mechanical Damage
The shell is thin, and eggs may therefore be damaged by:
For this reason, the egg should be collected together with a small section of the leaf rather than being removed directly.
Sensitivity to Insecticides
Eggs occur on the plant surface and can therefore be directly exposed to spray solution, but they do not respond equally to all insecticides.
Sensitivity depends on:

items: Presence of ovicidal activity in the active ingredient.
Ability of the material to penetrate the chorion.

items.text: Many insecticides used against Tuta are more effective against larvae than eggs. Therefore, spraying an insecticide that is effective against larvae does not necessarily eliminate all eggs present on the plant.
One Egyptian experiment recorded an ovicidal effect of a product based on azadirachtin or neem oil, reaching approximately:
43.8% egg mortality
Under the experimental conditions. This result demonstrates partial ovicidal activity but does not indicate complete egg control.
Sensitivity to Predators
Eggs are suitable prey for several predators present in tomato production systems, particularly certain predatory bugs, including:
Predators pierce the eggshell with their mouthparts and suck out its contents.
Predated eggs appear as:

items: Absence of a normal hatching opening.

items.text: Sensitivity to Egg Parasitoids
Eggs are direct targets of parasitoids belonging to the genus:
Trichogramma
Species used or studied against Tuta absoluta include:
The female parasitoid lays its egg inside the Tuta egg, after which the parasitoid larva feeds on the embryo and egg contents, preventing the emergence of a Tuta larva.
Parasitized eggs gradually become dark or black.
Success of parasitism varies according to:

items: Distribution of parasitoid cards or capsules.

items.text: Newly laid eggs are usually more suitable for parasitism than eggs approaching hatching.
Natural Non-Viability
Not all eggs laid by females hatch. Some may die naturally as a result of:
A field study of natural mortality factors showed that mortality during the egg stage reached approximately:
The principal causes were:
This result demonstrates that the egg stage is important for natural population regulation before larval damage occurs.
Importance of the Egg Stage in Integrated Pest Management
The egg stage represents one of the most important targets in the integrated management of Tuta absoluta for the following reasons:
The Egg Precedes Damage
The egg does not produce mines or holes. Therefore, killing or parasitizing it prevents damage before it begins.
Every egg prevented from hatching means:

items: A lower probability of fruit infestation.
Less pressure on the insecticide program.
A reduced population in the next generation.

items.text: The Egg Is Located Outside Plant Tissue
The egg is attached to the surface of the leaf, stem, or sepals, whereas the larva becomes concealed inside tissues after hatching.
Eggs are therefore more exposed to:
The Egg Determines the Timing of Larval Emergence
Examination of egg color and age allows estimation of hatching time.
New creamy eggs require more time, whereas the appearance of the black head means that larval emergence is imminent.
This information becomes important when timing:

items: Use of products with ovicidal activity.
Use of biological insecticides targeting newly hatched larvae.
Prediction of the appearance of new mines.

items.text: The Control Window Becomes Shorter Under Warm Conditions
At 20°C, the egg stage may persist for approximately 7–8 days, whereas eggs may hatch within 2.5–4 days at 30°C.
Therefore, the period available for intervention becomes shorter as temperature rises.

Under warm conditions, weekly inspection alone is not sufficient when trap catches are high and eggs are present, because a large proportion of eggs may hatch and larvae may enter leaves between two widely spaced inspections.
Supporting Biological Control
The egg stage is the principal target of Trichogramma parasitoids and egg predators.
The effectiveness of a Trichogramma program depends on the continued presence of the parasitoid in the field at the same time as newly laid Tuta eggs are present.
Releases made after most eggs have hatched do not control larvae that have already entered plant tissues.
Relationship Between the Egg and Larval Emergence
After embryonic development is completed, the larva chews through part of the eggshell and emerges onto the plant surface.
The newly hatched larva remains exposed for a short period before entering the tissue, and this period may be less than:
90 minutes under some conditions.
During this period, the larva is more exposed to:
Once it enters the leaf, stem, or fruit, the effectiveness of many external control methods declines.
Successful control is therefore linked to two successive stages:

items: 2. Targeting the larva immediately after hatching and before it enters plant tissues.

items.text: Distinguishing Among Egg Conditions

rows: A dark head visible at one end
Empty transparent or white shell with an emergence opening
Dry, malformed, or brown without normal development
Dark or black in a relatively uniform manner
Collapsed, empty, or bearing a fine puncture
Does not hatch and may change color or its contents may dry

items.text: Empty eggshells are not counted as live eggs when estimating infestation density.
Field Egg-Inspection Program
Accurate field inspection includes:

items: 2. Inspecting both field edges and the interior.
3. Focusing on the side adjacent to old fields.
4. Selecting plants distributed among several rows.
5. Inspecting growing points and young leaves.
6. Turning leaflets over to inspect the lower surface.
8. Inspecting sepals and calyces when fruits are present.
10. Classifying eggs as live, approaching hatching, parasitized, or empty.
11. Recording the number of plants carrying eggs.
12. Linking the results to moth numbers in traps.
13. Repeating inspection at shorter intervals when temperature rises.

items.text: The number of plants carrying eggs may sometimes describe the spread of infestation more clearly than the total number of eggs on a single heavily infested plant.
Collecting Eggs for Laboratory Examination
The egg should be collected together with a small piece of leaf by following these steps:

items: 1. Locate the egg using a hand lens.
2. Cut a section of the leaflet around it.
3. Place the specimen in a well-ventilated container.
4. Prevent water condensation inside the container.
5. Record the location, date, and cultivar.
6. Record the egg’s position on the plant.
8. Record emergence of a Tuta larva or egg parasitoid.
9. Do not remove the egg directly with forceps.
10. Do not expose it to sunlight or heat inside a vehicle.

items.text: Emergence of the parasitoid or larva is more accurate than color alone for determining parasitism and viability.
Comprehensive Reference Table for the Egg Stage

rows: Darker, with appearance of the black head
Usually singly or in small groups
Preventing larval emergence before tissue penetration

items.text: Summary of the Egg Stage

The egg of Tuta absoluta is extremely small and oval or elliptical in shape, measuring approximately 0.35–0.38 mm in length and 0.20–0.22 mm in width. When laid, it is creamy white or pale yellow, then becomes progressively darker as the embryo develops until the black head appears before hatching.

The female lays approximately 73% of eggs on leaves, 21% on veins, petioles, and stems, 5% on sepals, and 1% on green fruits. Eggs are commonly concentrated on young leaves and in the upper part of the plant, with greater aggregations at field edges during the early stages of infestation.

The incubation period usually ranges from 4 to 6 days under suitable conditions, but it is strongly affected by temperature. It may extend to approximately 12 days at 15°C, decline to approximately 4.5 days at 25°C, and to 2.5–4 days at 30°C.

The most suitable and stable thermal range lies between 20 and 25°C, and 25°C is considered one of the best temperatures for egg development because it combines rapid hatching, high viability, and the ability of subsequent stages to complete development.
Results at 30–35°C vary among studies. Rapid hatching may occur, but overall survival of the generation declines, especially when high temperature persists or is accompanied by low humidity.
Eggs are also affected by desiccation, extreme heat, cold, insecticides with ovicidal activity, predators, Trichogramma parasitoids, and natural non-viability.

The egg stage is a principal target in integrated pest management because it occurs outside plant tissues and precedes the onset of damage. Killing or parasitizing the egg prevents larval emergence and penetration into leaves or fruits, thereby reducing damage, larval density, and the next pest generation.
Part Three: The Larva
Larval Instars, Diagnostic Characteristics, Behavior, and Applied Importance
Introduction

The larva represents the damaging stage in the life cycle of Tuta absoluta. It is the only stage that feeds directly on tomato plant tissues and forms mines within leaves, growing points, stems, and fruits.

The egg, pupa, and adult moth do not cause direct mining damage. The egg represents the stage of embryonic development, the pupa is the transformation stage, while the adult moth is responsible for mating, dispersal, and egg laying.
The larva passes through four successive instars:
These instars are separated by three molts. At the end of the fourth instar, the larva enters the prepupal stage, stops feeding, and searches for a suitable site in which to pupate.
The four instars differ in:

items: Ability to move between plant parts.
Depth of position within plant tissues.
Level of protection from insecticides and natural enemies.

items.text: Newly hatched larvae consume less tissue, but they establish the infestation by entering the plant. Advanced larvae consume greater quantities, form wider mines, and are more capable of attacking growing points, stems, and fruits.
General Appearance of the Larva
The larva of Tuta absoluta is a lepidopteran larva with an elongated cylindrical body divided into relatively distinct segments.
The body consists of:

items: Abdomen composed of approximately ten visible segments.
Three pairs of true thoracic legs.
Fleshy abdominal prolegs that assist movement.
Fine setae distributed over the body.
Small cuticular plates or areas bearing the setae.

items.text: The head is characterized by being:

items: Distinct and separated from the body.
Increasing in size as the larva passes from one instar to the next.

items.text: The hardened layer covering the head is known as the:
Head Capsule

Head-capsule width is one of the most accurate indicators used to distinguish larval instars because body length changes temporarily as the larva contracts or extends, whereas head measurement is more stable within a given instar.
Parts of the Larval Body
Head
The head bears:

items: Strong chewing mandibles relative to larval size.
Mouthparts adapted for biting plant tissues.
Sensory setae that assist in selecting feeding sites.

items.text: The larva uses its mandibles to:

items: Penetrate growing points, stems, and fruits.
Open an exit route from the mine or move to another site.

items.text: The head is distinctly dark in the first instar, then ranges from brown to dark brown or partially black in advanced instars.
Thorax
The thorax consists of three segments, each bearing one pair of true legs, giving a total of:
Three pairs — six true legs.
On the first thoracic segment, immediately behind the head, there is a sclerotized plate known as the:
Prothoracic Shield
The color pattern of this shield is an important diagnostic feature in advanced larvae.

In Tuta absoluta, the shield is relatively pale, with a dark shading or band along its posterior margin. This band becomes more distinct in advanced instars, whereas it may be absent or weak in very young larvae.
Abdomen
The abdomen consists of successive segments, some of which bear fleshy abdominal prolegs.
The abdominal prolegs do not resemble the jointed thoracic legs, but they help the larva to:
The body contains lateral respiratory openings known as:
Spiracles
These permit air to enter the tracheal system.
Setae and Seta-Bearing Areas
Fine sensory setae occur over the larval body and arise from small cuticular areas known as:
Pinacula
These setae help the larva detect:
These characteristics are not easy to observe during field inspection, but they are used in microscopic diagnosis and in comparisons between Tuta absoluta and closely related species.
Number of Larval Instars
Tuta absoluta usually passes through four distinct larval instars.
The larva moves from one instar to the next through molting, during which:

items: 2. The old epidermis separates from the new epidermis.
4. The larva emerges with a new, soft covering.
5. The body expands before the new covering hardens.

items.text: Three molts occur during the larval stage:

items: First molt between L1 and L2.
Second molt between L2 and L3.
Third molt between L3 and L4.

items.text: After completing L4, the larva does not enter a fifth instar; instead, it transforms into the prepupal stage and then the pupa.
Instars are accurately determined using:

items: Body size and color as supporting indicators.

items.text: Body length alone is not a decisive criterion because it is affected by movement, feeding, preservation method, environmental conditions, and host plant.
First Larval Instar, L1
Appearance and Color
The first-instar larva emerges from the egg as a small and delicate larva with a body that is:

items: Provided with a distinct dark-brown or black head.

items.text: After feeding begins, the contents of the digestive tract may be visible through the translucent body.
The dark band behind the head is not always visible in this instar. Therefore, its absence in a newly hatched larva does not exclude Tuta absoluta.
Measurements
Published measurements vary according to the study and the method used to collect larvae.
The length of the first instar in different studies ranges approximately between:
0.6 and 1.5 mm.
Head-capsule width usually ranges around:
One study recorded a range of 0.16–0.22 mm and a mean of 0.18 mm.
The relatively wide variation in body length results from the fact that some measurements are taken immediately after hatching, whereas others are taken near the end of the first instar.
Behavior After Hatching
After emerging from the egg, the larva begins searching for a suitable point at which to penetrate the plant.
It may move over the leaf surface for a short period before biting through the epidermis and entering the tissue.
Preferred sites include:

items: At the bases of plant trichomes.
Within folds of the leaf surface.
Areas near the calyx or growing points.

items.text: The period before the larva enters the tissue is one of the most vulnerable periods in its life because it is exposed on the plant surface and is not yet protected within a mine.
Once entry is completed, the larva becomes positioned between the two epidermal layers of the leaf and begins consuming internal tissue.
Nature of Damage
The newly hatched larva forms a small and delicate mine that may appear as:

items: A mining point that is difficult to detect.
An area containing a small amount of frass.

items.text: Damage caused by each individual larva is initially limited, but the importance of L1 lies in establishing the infestation site, which expands as the larva grows.
Duration of the First Instar
In one laboratory study, the duration of the first instar ranged between:
Two and three days
With a mean of approximately:
2.08 days.
However, this period varies according to temperature, host, cultivar, humidity, and the nutritional condition of the larva.
Importance in Control
The first instar is one of the most important control targets because:

items: It remains exposed for a short period after hatching.
It has not yet consumed a large quantity of tissue.
It is generally located close to the leaf surface.
It requires less food to ingest a lethal dose of some ingestion-active products.
It has a lower capacity to move between plant parts.
It generally has not yet entered fruits or deep stem tissues.

items.text: However, the window for targeting this instar is short because the larva enters plant tissues rapidly after hatching.
Second Larval Instar, L2
Appearance and Color
After the first molt, the larva increases in size and the body becomes:

items: Provided with a black or dark-brown head.
More clearly visible within the mine.

items.text: Green coloration begins to appear as the larva feeds on plant tissues and the digestive tract fills with green plant material.
Measurements
The length of the second instar in different studies ranges approximately between:
1.6 and 2.7 mm.
Head-capsule width is approximately:
One study recorded a range of 0.24–0.35 mm and a mean of 0.30 mm.
The clear increase in head width allows L2 to be distinguished from L1 more reliably than by body color alone.
Behavior and Feeding
Most second-instar larvae remain inside leaves, but they become more capable of:

items: Moving between parts of the leaflet.

items.text: Dark frass becomes more visible within the mine, and the larva is easier to observe when the leaf is held against the light.
Duration of the Second Instar
In one study, the duration of the second instar ranged between:
Two and four days
With a mean of approximately:
2.75 days.
The period becomes longer under cool conditions and shorter under warm temperatures within the suitable range.
Importance in Control
The second instar remains a relatively suitable control target, particularly when larvae are located within superficial leaf mines.
However, its survival capacity improves compared with the first instar, and the quantity of tissue consumed increases.
Control is more successful when the active ingredient reaches the mine or the larva is forced to ingest it during feeding, rather than relying only on contact with the outer leaf surface.
Third Larval Instar, L3
Appearance and Color
In the third instar, the body becomes:

items: Light or dark green depending on the food.

items.text: The dark band along the posterior margin of the prothoracic shield begins to become more visible.
The body color may shift to pinkish or brownish shades when feeding inside fruits or when the larva approaches the end of its development.
Measurements
The length of the third instar is generally approximately:
Head-capsule width ranges approximately between:
One study recorded a range of 0.35–0.45 mm and a mean of 0.38 mm.
Activity and Damage
The third instar becomes more active and consumes more food. It can:

items: Move from one leaflet to another.

items.text: The amount of frass inside mines increases and may appear as irregular dark aggregations.
Economic damage begins to increase clearly during this instar, particularly when larvae reach reproductive plant parts.
Duration of the Third Instar
In one study, the duration of the third instar ranged between:
Three and five days
With a mean of approximately:
3.56 days.
The duration varies according to temperature, cultivar, host, and the type of tissue on which the larva feeds.
Importance of the Third Instar in Control
Control of the third instar becomes more difficult than control of L1 and L2 because it:

items: Occurs within wider and deeper mines.
Can move into stems and fruits.
Consumes a larger amount of tissue.
May leave the leaf before treatment reaches it.
Has a larger body size and greater ability to tolerate inadequate doses.

items.text: This does not mean that control is impossible, but treatment success depends more heavily on:

items: Ability of the insecticide to penetrate plant tissues.

items.text: Fourth Larval Instar, L4
Appearance and Color
The fourth instar is the largest and most conspicuous larval instar.
Body color ranges between:

items: Pinkish brown near the prepupal stage.

items.text: The head is dark brown, and the dark band along the posterior margin of the prothoracic shield becomes more distinct.
Pink bands or areas may appear on the dorsal surface of the body, particularly in fully grown larvae.
Measurements
The length of the fourth instar in several studies is approximately:
The fully grown larva may reach approximately:
In some guides and geographical strains.
Head-capsule width usually measures approximately:
One study recorded a range of 0.53–0.63 mm and a mean of 0.58 mm.
Body width in some measurements reaches approximately 1.1 mm.
Feeding and Damage
The fourth instar consumes the largest amount of tissue compared with earlier instars and can cause:

items: Collapse of large areas of the leaf.
Increased probability of entry by rot pathogens.

items.text: The larva may leave an old mine and move to a new location. Therefore, empty mines may be present while active infestation continues elsewhere.
Large larvae may also be observed hanging from silken threads during movement or while searching for a pupation site.
Duration of the Fourth Instar
In one study, the duration of the fourth instar ranged between:
Three and five days
With a mean of approximately:
3.86 days.
The final part of this instar includes the period in which the larva becomes fully grown and prepares to stop feeding.
End of the Fourth Instar
When development is completed:

items: The larva stops enlarging the mine.
Part of the digestive tract is emptied.
The body may become shorter and more robust.
The larva leaves the feeding site.
It searches for a protected pupation site.

items.text: Pupation may occur within:

items: A mine or protected plant part.
A silken cocoon on the leaf.

items.text: Table of Larval Instar Measurements
Measurements vary according to strain, host, temperature, and exact stage at measurement. Therefore, the following values represent approximate practical ranges:

rows: White or creamy with a dark head
Green to pink or pinkish brown

items.text: One morphometric study recorded the following means:
A small difference in length alone should not be used to determine the instar; the head, cast skin, color, and behavior should be examined together.
Change in Larval Color
Larval color is not a fixed characteristic and is not sufficient by itself for diagnosis.
Color is affected by:

items: Exposure to an insecticide or pathogen.

items.text: The larva remains pale for several hours after molting because the new covering has not yet acquired its final color and hardness.
Advanced larvae may become:

items: Pink when the body is fully fed or approaching pupation.
Brown or dark when diseased or dead.

items.text: Therefore, observing a pink larva does not mean that it is a different species, and observing a green larva does not confirm that it is Tuta.
Dark Band Behind the Head
One of the most important characters used to identify Tuta absoluta larvae is the presence of a dark band along the posterior margin of the prothoracic shield immediately behind the head.
This band appears as:

items: A transverse band behind the head.

items.text: It is more distinct in the third and fourth instars, whereas it may be absent or weak in young larvae.
However, this character is not definitive by itself because tomato pinworm:
Keiferia lycopersicella
Also possesses a dark band behind the head, although it is generally less distinct.
Reliable differentiation between the two species requires examination of additional characters and may involve:
Molting and Head Capsules
During transition between instars, the larva leaves behind its old external covering. The mine may contain:

items: Frass accumulated near the molting site.

items.text: The old head capsule is dark and relatively hard and may be used to determine the previous instar.
Finding more than one head capsule within a mine may indicate that the larva spent several instars in the same area, but does not necessarily mean that several larvae were present.
Larval movement and feeding temporarily decline before molting and resume after the new covering hardens.
Mining Behavior Within Leaves
The larva feeds between the two epidermal layers of the leaf, consuming mesophyll tissue while part of the outer epidermis remains intact.
The mine begins small and then expands as the larva grows, becoming:

items: A dry brown blotch in old infestation.

items.text: Dark excreta known as:
Frass
Are commonly present within the mine.
The frass may accumulate in one part of the mine rather than being distributed evenly.
Tuta mines are generally broad and blotch-like, whereas mines formed by Liriomyza flies are usually narrower, winding, or serpentine.
However, several overlapping Liriomyza mines may form a broad area. Therefore, the mine should be opened and the larva itself examined.
Movement of Larvae Between Feeding Sites
The larva does not necessarily remain inside the first mine throughout the larval stage.
It may leave and move to:

items: A new site within the same leaf.

items.text: The larva uses a silken thread to assist in:
Large larvae may hang from the plant by a silken thread, particularly when plants are shaken or the feeding site is disturbed.
Infestation of Growing Points and Stems
When the larva enters the growing point or a tender stem, it may cause:

items: Wilting of the plant part beyond the infestation site.
Reduced fruit set and early yield.

items.text: Transplants and young plants are more sensitive to growing-point infestation because loss of the main apex represents a large proportion of the plant’s vegetative structure.
Frass may be present at the entry hole or inside the infested bud.
Fruit Infestation
Larvae enter fruits from different sites. Entry is commonly found:

items: At points where a leaf contacts the fruit.

items.text: The larva leaves:
The size of the entry hole does not reflect the extent of internal damage. The hole may be minute while the tunnel extends through a large area of the fruit.
Fruit infestation is particularly serious because it converts partially compensable leaf damage into direct loss of marketable or processable yield.
Duration of the Larval Stage
The duration of the larval stage changes greatly according to temperature.
In one study conducted under constant temperatures, the total duration of the larval stage was as follows:
These values demonstrate that cold conditions do not necessarily prevent feeding, but they prolong the period during which larvae remain on the plant.
In other studies conducted under warm conditions, the total duration generally ranged around:
10–14 days
And may extend for longer periods depending on temperature, cultivar, and host.
A shorter duration at high temperature does not always mean greater damage by an individual larva because daily food intake, survival, and physiological condition also change.
Most Suitable Temperatures for Larval Development
Development becomes faster as temperature increases within the suitable range, but survival does not continue to increase at extreme temperatures.
In one study:
Thermal studies indicate that a range of approximately:
Provides an appropriate balance among:

items: Successful transformation to the pupal stage.

items.text: Temperatures around 30°C shorten the duration of the stage, but the overall effect depends on exposure duration, humidity, strain, and host plant.
Extremely high or low temperatures cause:

items: Development of abnormal larvae or pupae.

items.text: Effect of Host Plant and Cultivar
Larvae do not develop at the same rate on all plants or tomato cultivars.
Their performance is affected by:
Studies on processing tomato cultivars recorded larval durations ranging from less than nine days on some cultivars to more than twelve days on others.
Larval duration alone is not sufficient to evaluate cultivar resistance. The following should also be measured:
Sensitivity of Larvae to Different Factors
Desiccation
Larvae inside mines are relatively protected from dry air because the leaf provides a more humid microclimate.
However, larvae become more exposed to desiccation when they:

items: Are newly hatched before penetrating the tissue.

items.text: High temperature and dry air increase water loss, especially in small larvae.
Rainfall and Overhead Irrigation
Water droplets do not easily reach larvae established inside mines, but they may affect exposed larvae by:

items: Increasing humidity and encouraging some entomopathogens.
Removing a proportion of newly hatched larvae.

items.text: Overhead irrigation or rainfall is not a sufficient control method because a large proportion of larvae remain protected within plant tissues.
Predators
Larvae, especially young larvae close to the mine surface, are preyed upon by organisms including:
Predators may pierce the leaf epidermis and suck the larva located inside the mine or capture it while it is leaving and moving.
Larval Parasitoids
Different parasitoids attack Tuta larvae, including species belonging to groups such as:
The suitable larval instar for parasitism varies according to parasitoid species.
The parasitoid may lay its egg:

items: Beside the larva within the mine after paralyzing it.

items.text: Excessive use of broad-spectrum insecticides kills these parasitoids and reduces their natural contribution.
Entomopathogenic Bacteria
Products containing Bacillus thuringiensis act after the larva ingests the crystals and bacteria deposited on treated tissue.
Young instars are generally more suitable targets because they:

items: Have not yet entered fruits or deep stem tissues.
Are less capable of tolerating intestinal damage.

items.text: Efficacy declines when:

items: The material does not reach the feeding site.
The product degrades under radiation and environmental conditions.
Resistance or reduced susceptibility is present.

items.text: Entomopathogenic Fungi
Fungi such as Beauveria bassiana and Metarhizium anisopliae can infect larvae when conidia reach the body and suitable humidity permits germination.
Exposed larvae or larvae moving outside mines are more likely to come into contact with the fungi, whereas direct contact is less likely for larvae deep inside fruits.
Entomopathogenic Nematodes
Some entomopathogenic nematodes can reach larvae inside mines when a film of moisture allows their movement.
Their efficacy is associated with:
Chemical Insecticides
Larval susceptibility to insecticides varies according to:

items: Mode of action of the insecticide.
Ability of the material to penetrate the leaf.
History of use of the insecticide group in the field.

items.text: Young instars are generally easier to target than large larvae located inside fruits and stems.
Changing the trade name does not mean changing the mode of action because several products may belong to the same IRAC group.
Why Are Advanced Larvae More Difficult to Control?
Control becomes more difficult from L1 to L4 for the following reasons:

items: Larger body size and greater ability to tolerate an incomplete dose.
Increased infestation of fruits and stems.
Reduced direct exposure of the body to spray solution.
Ability to leave the treated mine.
Rapid production of damage before death.
Possible resistance to the active ingredient.
Reduced efficacy of some biological insecticides against large instars.

items.text: Therefore, program efficacy is not measured only by its ability to kill L4, but by its ability to prevent a large proportion of the pest population from reaching that stage.
Distinguishing Tuta Larvae from Liriomyza Larvae

rows: Not the usual form of damage

items.text: When there is uncertainty, the mine should be opened carefully. The presence of a distinct head and legs confirms that the larva is lepidopteran and not a leaf-mining fly larva.
Distinguishing Tuta from Tomato Fruitworm
The larva of Tuta absoluta differs from Helicoverpa armigera in several characteristics:

rows: Inside the mine or near the opening
Larger quantities near the fruit opening

items.text: Distinguishing Tuta absoluta from Tomato Pinworm
Tuta absoluta resembles tomato pinworm, Keiferia lycopersicella, in:

items: Presence of a dark band behind the head.

items.text: For this reason, reliable separation based on the larva or damage alone may be difficult.
The dark marking behind the head is usually stronger in Tuta, but final diagnosis in regions where both species occur may require specialist examination.
Field Examination of Larvae
Field examination begins with recent mines rather than only with dry leaves.
The following steps are used:

items: 1. Select plants from field edges and the interior.
4. Hold the leaf against the light to locate the larva.
5. Open the mine using a fine needle.
6. Record the presence or absence of a live larva.
8. Examine the head and prothoracic band.
10. Inspect growing points, stems, and fruits.
11. Do not count an old empty mine as active infestation.
12. Record the number of infested plants, not only the number of mines.

items.text: Assessment should distinguish among:

items: New mines containing L1 or L2.
Wide active mines containing L3 or L4.

items.text: Collection and Preservation of Larvae
The larva should be collected together with the plant tissue in which it occurs because forceful removal may tear it.
The collection method includes:

items: Placing it in a well-ventilated container.
Adding a fresh leaf when necessary.
Recording the location, date, and cultivar.
Separating fruit-infesting larvae from leaf samples.
Avoiding exposure of containers to sunlight.
Transferring samples rapidly to the examination site.

items.text: When preserving a specimen for morphological diagnosis, an appropriate preservation method should be used to avoid loss of body color or deformation of the head capsule.
Applied Importance of Larval Instars
The four instars are not equal in their importance for decision-making.

rows: Beginning of penetration or a small mine
Wide mines, growing points, stems, and fruits
Deep mines, fruits, and movement toward pupation

items.text: A successful program focuses on:

items: Preventing hatching as much as possible.
Reducing the number of larvae reaching L3 and L4.
Removing sources of larvae and pupae at the end of the season.

items.text: Reference Table for the Larval Stage

rows: Yellowish green, bluish green, or pink
Approximately 7.2–7.6 mm and may reach 9–10 mm
Dark band behind the head in advanced instars
Leaves, growing points, stems, and fruits
Exit and search for a pupation site

items.text: Summary of the Larval Stage
The larva is the stage responsible for all direct damage caused by Tuta absoluta. It passes through four instars separated by three molts.
The first-instar larva emerges small, white or creamy, and with a dark head, then rapidly enters plant tissue. The body gradually increases in size and becomes green or pink during advanced instars.

Approximate instar lengths range from less than one millimeter to approximately 7.5 mm, while the fully grown larva may reach approximately 9–10 mm. Head-capsule width is more accurate than body length for determining larval instar.

Advanced larvae are characterized by a dark band behind the head along the posterior margin of the prothoracic shield, but this character alone is not sufficient to distinguish them from some closely related species.
Larvae begin by mining leaves and then become increasingly capable of attacking growing points, petioles, stems, and fruits. They form irregular blotch mines that commonly contain dark frass.

The larval stage lasts approximately 10–14 days under suitable warm conditions but may extend beyond one month under cold conditions. The most suitable thermal range for larval development and survival is approximately 20–25°C.
Control becomes more difficult as larval age advances because large larvae enter deeper tissues, cause greater damage, and become less exposed to insecticides and natural enemies.
Effective management therefore focuses on detecting eggs and recent mines and targeting L1 and L2 before larvae reach advanced instars and fruits.
Part Four: The Pupal Stage
Morphological Characteristics, Pupation Sites, Developmental Duration, Sensitivity, and Applied Importance
Introduction

The pupa represents the transitional stage between the larva and the adult insect in the life cycle of Tuta absoluta. It is a non-feeding stage; the pupa does not mine leaves, stems, or fruits and causes no direct damage to the plant.

However, the absence of feeding does not mean that this stage is economically unimportant. Every viable pupa is capable of producing an adult moth that emerges from the pupation site, mates, lays eggs, and initiates a new pest generation.
The importance of the pupal stage lies in the fact that it:

items: Represents the transition from the damaging stage to the reproductive and dispersal stage.
Is often located in protected sites that are not easily reached by foliar spray treatments.
Can survive in soil or crop residues after the crop has ended.
May be transported with crates, packages, and sorting residues.
Helps the pest persist between cropping seasons.
May contribute to pest survival during cold periods.
Represents a target for cultural practices directed at soil and crop residues.

items.text: Therefore, management of Tuta absoluta is not completed by controlling eggs and larvae alone, but must also include reducing pupal numbers and preventing adult moth emergence.
Prepupal Stage
A fully grown larva does not transform immediately into a pupa, but first passes through a short transitional stage known as the:
Prepupal Stage
This stage begins at the end of the fourth larval instar.
External Signs of the Prepupal Stage
As the larva approaches pupation, several clear changes appear, including:

items: The larva leaves the mine or fruit in most cases.
Color changes from green to light green or pink.
Part of the digestive-tract contents is evacuated.
Movement increases while searching for a suitable site.
The larva hides in a folded leaf, crevice, or surface soil.

items.text: At this stage, the larva is less closely associated with the feeding site, and it may therefore be found far from the mine it created.
Searching for a Pupation Site
The fully grown larva leaves the feeding site and moves to a location that provides:

items: A surface on which the cocoon can be secured.
Reduced risk of flooding or mechanical disturbance.

items.text: The larva may move across the plant or descend to the soil on a silken thread, then enter between soil particles or beneath surface residues.
Difference Between the Pupa and the Cocoon
The terms pupa and cocoon are commonly confused, although they do not have the same meaning.
Pupa
The pupa is the insect’s own body during transformation from larva to adult moth.
Inside it, the following processes occur:

items: Breakdown and reorganization of larval tissues.
Formation of the adult reproductive system.
Completion of the moth’s body form.

items.text: Cocoon
The cocoon is the external silken covering spun by the larva around itself before transforming into a pupa.
The cocoon may consist of:
Therefore:
> The pupa is the insect itself, whereas the cocoon is the covering surrounding it.
The pupa is not always enclosed within a dense, clearly visible cocoon; the silken covering may be thin or nearly transparent, particularly when pupation occurs on leaves.
General Appearance of the Pupa
The pupa of Tuta absoluta is of the type known as:
Obtect Pupa
In this type, the developing adult appendages, such as the wings, legs, and antennae, are fixed close to the body surface and are not freely movable.
The pupa is characterized by:

items: A narrower and more tapered posterior end.
Distinct wing cases on both sides of the thorax and abdomen.
Leg and antennal sheaths attached closely to the body.
A posterior structure that assists anchorage inside the cocoon.

items.text: The pupa remains motionless most of the time, but it may move the abdominal segments when touched or disturbed, particularly during the early part of the pupal period.
Parts of the Pupal Body
Although the external boundaries are less distinct than in the larva or moth, the main body regions can still be recognized.
Head Region
The head is located at the anterior end, where the following can be observed:

items: Positions of the developing compound eyes.
General outline of the head compressed against the thorax.

items.text: Thorax
The thorax carries structures that will develop into:

items: Muscles required for flight and movement.

items.text: The wing cases appear as long, smooth areas extending from the thorax toward the abdomen.
Abdomen
The abdomen appears to consist of successive segments, with the posterior segments being more distinct and more mobile.
Anchoring structures occur at the posterior end and help keep the pupa secured within the cocoon and prevent slipping as the moth moves during emergence.
Pupal Size
The average length of a Tuta absoluta pupa is approximately:
In many field descriptions, the general length is close to:
Approximately 5 mm.
Female pupae are larger than male pupae on average.
Measurements of Male Pupae
Measurements of Female Pupae
These measurements alone should not be used to determine sex because large males may overlap in size with small females.
Pupal dimensions are affected by:

items: Larval exposure to insecticides or diseases.

items.text: Pupal Weight and Its Significance
Pupal weight reflects the quantity of nutritional resources stored by the larva before pupation.
Pupae produced from well-fed larvae are usually:

items: More capable of producing healthy moths.
More likely to produce highly fecund females.

items.text: Small or low-weight pupae may result from:

items: Exposure to a sublethal insecticide dose.

items.text: Small pupal size does not necessarily mean mortality, but it may affect the characteristics, fecundity, and longevity of the resulting moth.
Change in Pupal Color
Pupal color changes clearly as internal development progresses.
Newly Formed Pupa
During the first hours, the pupa is:
At this stage, the outer covering has not yet hardened completely.
Mid-Pupal Period
The color gradually changes to:
The covering becomes more sclerotized, and details of the wings and segments become clearer.
Near Adult Emergence
Before adult emergence, the pupa becomes:
The dark eyes and wings of the developing moth become visible through the pupal covering.
Gradual darkening before emergence is a normal change, but it differs from irregular blackening caused by death or fungal infection.
Live and Dead Pupae
Indicators of a Live Pupa

items: The body remains full and is not shrunken.
No odor of decomposition is present.
A healthy moth emerges at the end of the developmental period.

items.text: Indicators of a Dead Pupa

items: Fixed black coloration unrelated to moth development.
White, green, or dark fungal growth.
No moth emergence after the expected period has passed.

items.text: Dark coloration alone is not sufficient to determine pupal death because a healthy pupa naturally becomes dark before adult emergence.
Pupation Sites
Tuta absoluta can pupate in several locations. Selection of the pupation site depends on:

items: Proximity of the larva to the soil.
Degree of disturbance to the plant.

items.text: Pupation sites include:

items: 10. In packages, crates, and packing materials.

items.text: Pupation in Soil
A proportion of fully grown larvae leave the plant and descend to the soil surface, then enter the upper soil layer.
Pupae are usually found:

items: Within approximately the upper 1–2 cm of soil.
Among sand particles and loose soil.

items.text: The larva spins a silken cocoon to which soil particles may adhere, making its color resemble the surrounding medium and rendering detection difficult.
Pupation in soil increases when:

items: The crop approaches the end of the season.
Soil is loose and moderately moist.
Plants are disturbed by movement or harvesting.
Larvae descend from the canopy on silken threads.

items.text: Pupation on the Plant
Larvae can pupate on the canopy inside a thin silken cocoon.
Common sites include:

items: A fold along the leaf margin.
Beneath sepals or in other protected sites.

items.text: The larva spins silken threads that close the space or secure the leaf margins around its body.
The cocoon may be:

items: Mixed with frass or plant fragments.
Difficult to see without lifting the leaf or opening the fold.

items.text: Pupation in Processing and Fresh-Market Tomatoes
The distribution of pupation sites varies according to the production system.
In some processing tomato fields, soil becomes more important as a pupation site because of:

items: Branches being close to the soil surface.

items.text: In some fresh-market and greenhouse crops, a greater proportion of pupae may be found:

There is no single fixed pupation site for all production systems. Some field studies recorded higher numbers on leaves than in soil, whereas other observations showed a predominance of ground pupation.
Pupation in Containers and Handling Areas
Pupation is not restricted to the field. Larvae may leave fruits or accompanying leaves during:

items: Arrival of fruits at the market or processing plant.

items.text: They may then pupate in:
This characteristic allows the pest to move into new locations because the adult moth may emerge after the package has reached an area far from the original field.
Cocoon Formation
The larva produces silken threads from the silk glands, then moves its head and body to construct a covering around itself.
The process proceeds through the following stages:

items: 2. Attachment of initial threads to the substrate.
4. Incorporation of leaf fragments or soil particles.
8. Appearance of the pupa inside the cocoon.

items.text: The cocoon may be relatively strong when pupation occurs in soil, where silk becomes mixed with sand particles, whereas it is thinner when pupation occurs on leaves.
Duration of the Pupal Stage
The duration of the pupal stage depends primarily on temperature.
Under moderate and warm conditions, the pupal stage generally lasts approximately:
6–10 days.
However, it may extend for several weeks under cold conditions.

items.text: These values demonstrate a clear inverse relationship between temperature and pupal duration:
> The higher the temperature within the suitable range, the shorter the period required for transformation into the adult moth.
However, a shorter duration does not always mean that the highest temperature is optimal, because extreme heat may reduce survival or the quality of emerging moths.

Cold Tolerance
The pupa is one of the important stages for pest survival during cold periods, particularly when protected within soil, crop residues, or greenhouses.
A study of continuous cold exposure showed that pupae were able to survive at:
5°C for 30 or 60 days
And subsequently produced moths after being returned to suitable conditions, although emergence declined as exposure duration increased.
After continuous exposure for 90 days, some pupae appeared to remain alive, but no complete adult moths emerged.
Under a gradual cooling program:

items: Approximately 47% of moths emerged after a period at 11°C.
Emergence declined to approximately 12% during the following period at 10°C.
No emergence occurred when temperature was subsequently lowered to 8°C.

items.text: These results show that pupae can tolerate extended cold exposure, but not indefinitely, and their ability to complete development declines as the duration and intensity of cold increase.
Effect of Soil Moisture
Pupae require moderate moisture to prevent desiccation, but they are damaged by excessive water saturation.
Soil moisture affects:

Effect of Desiccation
Severe desiccation causes water loss from the pupa, particularly when it occurs:

items: In packages exposed to air and heat.

items.text: A pupa damaged by desiccation may appear as:

items: Emergence of a deformed or weak moth.

items.text: The cocoon and surface soil provide partial protection from desiccation, but they do not completely prevent water loss.
Effect of High Temperature
Pupal duration becomes shorter as temperature rises to 30°C within the tolerable range, but prolonged high temperature or temperatures above the upper threshold cause:

items: Failure of transformation into the adult.

items.text: Pupae inside soil are less exposed to temperature fluctuations than pupae located on leaves or exposed containers.

Distinguishing Male and Female Pupae
Pupal sex can be determined by examining the ventral surface under a stereomicroscope.
Female Pupa
It is characterized by:

items: A longitudinal genital opening or slit.
Located in the middle of the eighth abdominal segment.
Larger size and weight on average.

It is characterized by:

Absence of the female slit on the eighth segment.
A more slender body on average.

items.text: In routine field inspection, determining the sex of every pupa is not necessary.
Pupal Sensitivity to Insecticides
The pupa is a non-feeding stage and therefore is not affected by insecticides that require ingestion in the same manner as larvae.
The efficacy of many foliar sprays against pupae is also reduced because pupae:

items: Do not feed on treated tissue.

items.text: Some materials may act through contact, fumigation, or penetration, but success depends on direct delivery of the material to the pupa.
A spray that is effective against larvae should not automatically be considered effective against pupae present in the soil.

Pupae are exposed to varying degrees to:
However, a pupa enclosed within a cocoon and located inside soil is relatively protected.
The efficacy of biological agents is affected by:

Some experiments have shown that infection of Tuta pupae by entomopathogenic nematodes is possible, but mortality rates may be lower than those against larvae. Therefore, a management program should not rely on this method alone.
Importance of the Pupal Stage in Infestation Persistence
Pupae may remain present after:

items: Part of the crop has been removed.
Produce has been transported from the field.

Disposal of Crop Residues

Agricultural sanitation therefore includes:

Avoiding the accumulation of plant piles near the field.

Tillage after crop removal contributes to:

Moving pupae to depths that impede moth emergence.
Exposing them to birds and predators.

items.text: However, tillage efficacy varies with depth, soil type, and moisture, and shallow tillage alone does not guarantee complete pupal control.

However, understanding pupal sensitivity to severe desiccation and saturation helps explain changes in pupal numbers under different irrigation systems and soil textures.
