items.text: Summary of the Pupal Stage

The pupa is the non-feeding stage within which the larva of Tuta absoluta transforms into an adult moth. It is elongated or spindle-shaped and measures approximately 4.3–4.7 mm in length, with female pupae being larger than male pupae on average.
A newly formed pupa is green or yellowish green, then turns brown and becomes dark before adult emergence.

The insect pupates in soil, on leaves, inside folded leaves, within mines and crop residues, and in containers. Soil pupae are commonly located in the upper soil layer inside a silken cocoon to which sand particles adhere.
Pupal duration is affected by temperature. It is approximately 36.8 days at 10°C, declines to 10.2 days at 20°C, 8.4 days at 25°C, and 5.4 days at 30°C.
A range of 20–25°C provides suitable conditions for pupal development and adult emergence, while pupae can tolerate extended cold periods, especially in protected sites.
Pupae require moderate moisture. Both severe desiccation and water saturation reduce survival and emergence.
Most foliar spray treatments do not reach pupae located inside soil or cocoons. Their management therefore depends mainly on:

items: Shredding and tilling fields after harvest.
Use of appropriate biological agents within an integrated program.

items.text: The pupal stage causes no direct damage, but neglecting it allows new adult moths to emerge and initiate a new infestation cycle in the same field or in neighboring crops.
Part Five: The Adult Insect “Moth”
Morphological Characteristics, Sexual Differences, Activity, Reproduction, and Applied Importance
Introduction
The adult insect represents the final stage in the life cycle of Tuta absoluta. It appears as a small grayish-brown moth with a slender body and narrow wings covered with scales.
The adult moth does not produce the mines or holes found in leaves, stems, and fruits because direct damage is caused by the larvae. The principal functions of the adult stage are:

items: Expanding the wings and acquiring the ability to fly.

items.text: Its economic importance results from its capacity for reproduction and dispersal. A single moth does not consume the fruit or leaf, but a fertilized female lays enough eggs to initiate a new generation of mining larvae.
The adult insect is known in field usage by names such as:
The term Tuta moth is more entomologically accurate because it belongs to the micro-moths of the order Lepidoptera and is not one of the large diurnal butterflies.
Emergence of the Moth from the Pupa
When development of the insect inside the pupa is completed, the pupa becomes dark brown or blackish, and the developing moth structures become visible through the pupal covering.
Emergence then occurs in the following sequence:

items: 1. The adult insect separates from the inner pupal covering.
2. It moves the abdomen and legs inside the pupal shell.
3. It presses against the anterior end of the pupa.
4. The shell splits in the head and thoracic region.
5. The moth emerges with soft, folded wings.
6. It moves to a stable and suitable location.
7. Hemolymph is pumped into the wing veins.
9. The body dries and the legs and appendages harden.
10. The moth acquires its normal ability to move and fly.

items.text: The moth cannot fly efficiently immediately after emergence because the wings are small, soft, and folded around the body.
Wing expansion and hardening may require several hours depending on:

items: Space available around the emergence site.
Nutritional condition of the larva before pupation.
Exposure of the pupa to insecticides or heat stress.

items.text: If the moth emerges in a confined space or is compressed before the wings have fully expanded, it may develop twisted or shortened wings and have limited ability to fly and mate.
General Appearance of the Adult Insect
The Tuta absoluta moth is a small insect with a slender, elongated body. It has a mottled gray appearance that helps it remain concealed on dry leaves, stems, soil, and crop residues.
Its body consists of three main regions:
The adult insect bears:

items: One pair of upturned labial palpi.
Specialized genital structures at the end of the abdomen.

items.text: The general coloration is a mixture of:

items: Dark spots or dots on the forewings.

items.text: Color is not completely constant. The intensity of mottling and color shades may vary according to age, sex, geographical region, rearing conditions, and the condition of the scales.
Moth Size
Tuta absoluta is classified among the small moths.
Body length in a normal resting position is usually approximately:
Some guides may mention a measurement close to:
10 mm
When the entire insect is measured with the wings in a particular position or when a total length different from body length alone is used.
Forewing length is approximately:
Wingspan with the wings fully extended generally ranges around:
Measurement method explains much of the variation among published values. A researcher may measure:

items: Total length of the insect with wings folded.
Distance between the tips of the fully extended wings.

items.text: Females are larger and heavier than males on average, but there is clear size overlap between the sexes. Therefore, body length alone is not sufficient for sex determination.
Head
The moth’s head is small and covered with gray, brown, and pale scales.
It bears:

items: A number of sensory setae and scales.

items.text: The head may sometimes appear lighter than the thorax because of creamy or pale-yellow scales among the gray scales.
Compound Eyes
The moth possesses one pair of dark compound eyes on both sides of the head.
The eyes assist in:

The eyes are adapted to the moth’s crepuscular and nocturnal activity. However, vision is not the only mechanism used to locate the opposite sex. Chemical olfaction through the antennae plays the principal role in male response to the sex pheromone.
Antennae
The antennae are long, slender, and thread-like. This form is known as:
Filiform Antennae
Each antenna consists of a large number of small successive segments.
The antenna is nearly as long as the body or a substantial proportion of it and may appear banded with pale and dark rings because of the arrangement of scales on the segments.
The antennae perform important sensory functions, including:

items: Detecting chemical substances in the environment.

items.text: The male is highly sensitive to the components of the sex pheromone released by the female. This forms the basis of pheromone traps used for monitoring.
Male antennae are not strongly feathered as in some moth species; they remain filiform in appearance. Therefore, sex cannot be determined reliably in the field from antennal form alone.
Labial Palpi
Relatively long labial palpi occur at the front of the head and are directed upward. They are known as:
Upturned Labial Palpi
They are usually composed of three segments.
These palpi give the head a pointed or projecting appearance when the moth is viewed from the side.
The palpi bear setae and sensory receptors that help the insect examine its surroundings and detect chemical substances.
The upward direction of the palpi is an important general diagnostic feature in many moths belonging to the family Gelechiidae.
Mouthparts and Feeding
The moth does not possess chewing mouthparts like the larva and cannot mine leaves or penetrate fruits.
The ability of adult insects to utilize water or sugar solutions varies according to conditions. Longevity in laboratory rearing may increase when the following are provided:
However, the moth does not feed on plant tissues in a manner that causes direct economic loss. Its principal function remains reproduction and dispersal.
Thorax
The thorax consists of three segments:
It bears:

items: Muscles responsible for flight and movement.

items.text: The thorax is covered with gray, brown, and silvery scales and may also carry pale or faint yellow-orange scales.
The thorax is slightly wider than the abdomen but remains slender compared with many larger butterfly species.
Forewings
The forewings are narrow and elongated and are among the most important body parts used for external identification of the moth.
They are characterized by:

items: Intermingling of dark and pale scales.
Presence of small black spots or dots.
Presence of pale yellow or orange scales.
A rounded or slightly pointed tip.
Fringes along the posterior and terminal margins.

items.text: Dark spots are not always distributed in the same pattern among all individuals; they may be distinct in some moths and weak in others.
Wing appearance is affected by:
Old moths may become paler and less mottled because part of the scales has been lost.
Hindwings
The hindwings are:

items: Provided with a long margin of fine setae.

items.text: The long setae along the wing margins are known as:
Fringes
These fringes increase the effective aerodynamic surface of the wing without a major increase in insect weight, a common feature among small moths.
The hindwings are not clearly visible when the moth is at rest because they are covered by the forewings.
Scales
The moth’s wings and body are covered with minute scales, the feature from which the name of the order:
Lepidoptera
Is derived.
Scales perform functions that include:

items: Helping the insect escape from some surfaces or predators.

items.text: Scales detach easily when the wings are touched or when the moth contacts sticky traps.
For this reason, a moth intended for diagnosis should be held by the legs or body with fine instruments, and the wings should not be pressed with the fingers.
Legs
The moth has three pairs of legs attached to the three thoracic segments.
The legs are:

items: Banded or marked with pale and dark colors.
Equipped with fine claws at their ends.
Capable of gripping leaf and stem surfaces.

items.text: The legs are used for:
Sensory structures occur on the legs and help the moth examine plant surfaces before resting or laying eggs.
Abdomen
The abdomen is long, slender, and composed of successive segments.
It contains:

items: Glands associated with reproduction and pheromone release in the female.

items.text: The female abdomen is usually:

items: Capable of expansion when containing eggs.

items.text: The male abdomen is generally:

items: Equipped at its end with male copulatory structures.

items.text: These differences are not always sufficient for sex determination in small or damaged specimens.
Wing Position at Rest
When the moth rests on a leaf or stem, it folds its wings over the body in a roof-like or narrow covering position.
In this position:

items: The body appears long and slender.
The two wing margins meet above the back.
The antennae extend forward or to the sides.
The legs are positioned beneath the body.

items.text: The gray-brown coloration blends with:
This camouflage makes the moth difficult to detect during the day, particularly when it remains motionless within the canopy.
Differences Between Male and Female
Males and females are highly similar in color and wing shape. There is no strong and obvious sexual dimorphism that permits rapid sex determination by simple visual inspection.
General differences include:

rows: Not the principal source of sexual attraction
Strongly attracted to the female pheromone
Usually not captured by male-targeted sex traps
Laying eggs and establishing the generation

items.text: Size alone should not be relied upon because the sexes overlap.
Accurate sex determination depends on:

items: Internal characteristics of male and female genitalia.

items.text: Genitalia and Species Diagnosis
Examination of adult genitalia is among the most accurate morphological methods for distinguishing Tuta absoluta from closely related moths.
Male genitalia have specific structures, including:

items: A broad gnathos with a form resembling a horseshoe.
Other internal structures with species-specific forms.

items.text: The female also possesses diagnostic characteristics in:

items: Internal structures that differ from those of related species.

items.text: These characters cannot be seen with the naked eye and require:

items: 2. Treatment with suitable chemicals to remove soft tissues.
4. Mounting and spreading the genitalia on a slide.

items.text: This examination becomes important when closely related species are suspected, such as:

items: Other moths in the tribe Gnorimoschemini.

items.text: Molecular analysis may be used alongside morphological examination when definitive confirmation is required.
Daily Activity
The moth displays clear crepuscular and nocturnal activity.
During the day, it commonly hides:

items: In shaded areas of the greenhouse.
Between crates or in corners of structures.

items.text: Its activity generally increases:

items: During the first hours of darkness.

items.text: When plants are shaken during the day, moths may fly a short distance and then quickly return to hiding within the canopy.
This behavior explains why few moths may be observed visually despite high numbers being captured in traps.
Flight and Dispersal
The adult insect uses its wings for:

items: Moving among plants within the field.
Moving from old fields to newly planted fields.
Leaving fields after drying or harvest completion.

items.text: Dispersal occurs at two levels:
Local Dispersal
This takes place through flight among:
Long-Distance Dispersal
This is more strongly associated with human activity, such as:
Wind may assist moth movement, but it does not by itself explain rapid spread among countries and continents.
Mating and Sex Pheromone
The unmated female releases a volatile sex pheromone from glands near the tip of the abdomen.
The pheromone disperses in the air and is detected by the male through sensory receptors on the antennae.
The male then moves toward the odor source through:
Mating usually occurs during darkness or under low-light conditions.
The mating process includes:

items: 5. Transfer of sperm and associated substances.
7. Female oviposition after the pre-oviposition period.

items.text: The female may mate more than once, and multiple mating can increase reproductive output under some conditions. [3]
Pre-Oviposition Period
The period between female emergence and the beginning of egg laying is known as the:
Pre-oviposition Period
During this period:

items: Part of the egg complement matures.
The female searches for a suitable host plant.

items.text: This period generally lasts one or several days depending on:
The female then begins distributing eggs among plant parts, with a high proportion of oviposition occurring during the first days of adult life.
Oviposition Pattern During Female Life
The female does not lay the same number of eggs each day.
The usual pattern is:

items: High during the first days after mating.
Reaching a peak during the first few days.
Continuing at low levels until near the end of female life.

items.text: Field studies showed that females may lay approximately:
68–72% of their total eggs during the first five days.
In another behavioral study, oviposition peaked after the second and third days, and approximately:
72% of eggs were laid during the first seven days.

This rapid pattern means that the entry of a limited number of mated females into a recently planted field can result in the distribution of a large number of eggs before larval infestation is noticed.
Fecundity and Number of Eggs
The female of Tuta absoluta has a high reproductive capacity and can lay a large number of eggs on the aerial parts of tomato plants during its lifetime.
The commonly reported maximum in many references is approximately:
260 eggs per female.
Under favorable environmental and nutritional conditions, fecundity in some studies may rise to:
250–300 eggs per female.

This does not mean that every female lays 260 or 300 eggs. These values represent maximum reproductive capacity or high values recorded under suitable conditions. The actual mean number laid by a female is usually lower and may range from tens of eggs to more than 200 depending on conditions.
Fecundity is affected by:

items: Nutritional value of the tissues consumed by the larva.
Access of the adult to water or a sugar source.
Exposure to insecticides or heat stress.
Duration of the pre-oviposition and oviposition periods.

items.text: The female lays most of its eggs during the first days after mating. It may lay approximately 68–76% of the total egg complement during the first five to seven days, after which the daily oviposition rate gradually declines with age.

High fecundity, which may reach 260 eggs and may approach 300 eggs per female under optimum conditions, is one of the main reasons for the rapid multiplication of Tuta absoluta and the shift of infestation from a low level to a severe outbreak within a short period.
The severity of the pest population does not depend on egg number alone, but also on:

items: Overlap of pest stages within the field.

items.text: Three terms should therefore be distinguished:

rows: Mean number of eggs laid by a group of females
Total number of eggs laid by one female
Highest number recorded for a female under the study conditions

items.text: The reproductive capacity of Tuta absoluta can therefore be expressed scientifically as follows:
> A female lays a variable number of eggs. Maximum fecundity may reach approximately 260 eggs, while some references report a high range of 250–300 eggs per female under favorable conditions.
Adult Longevity
There is no single fixed lifespan for the adult moth of Tuta absoluta.
Recorded male and female longevity varies according to study conditions.
Under natural conditions:
In experiments on different tomato cultivars:

items: Adult longevity generally ranged around 10–16 days.
Some females lived for approximately 16 days or more.

items.text: Under cool laboratory conditions, adult longevity increased. One study recorded approximately:
Longer life at low temperature does not mean higher reproduction because activity, mating, and oviposition rates decline as temperature decreases.
Females live longer than males in many studies, but the result varies with food, temperature, mating, and strain.

Temperature affects:

Provides favorable conditions for survival of immature stages and female fecundity.
The optimum temperature for oviposition has been estimated at approximately:
Females can lay eggs over a wider thermal range, but reproductive performance is not equal at all temperatures.

Effect of Humidity and Desiccation
Dense foliage creates a more humid microclimate with less exposure to sunlight and wind.
Moths use this environment as a daytime refuge.
Severe desiccation leads to:

items: Movement of moths into shaded sites.
Increased mortality when combined with high temperature.

items.text: High humidity does not always mean higher moth abundance because humidity interacts with temperature, rainfall, ventilation, and entomopathogenic diseases.
Effect of Light and Ultraviolet Radiation
Moth behavior is affected by the alternation of light and darkness. Reproductive and locomotor activity increase during darkness or low-light periods.
Ultraviolet radiation also affects insect physiology.
Recent experiments showed that UV exposure reduced:

items: Female egg-laying capacity under severe exposure.

items.text: However, laboratory results based on concentrated radiation sources cannot be converted directly into field doses because natural exposure varies according to:

items: Glass or plastic used in greenhouses.

items.text: Effect of Wind and Rainfall
Wind affects moth movement in two ways:

items: It may assist passive dispersal under some conditions.
It may interfere with flight and female location when strong.

items.text: Wind may push moths toward particular field edges or sectors, sometimes explaining uneven trap catches.
Heavy rainfall may:

items: Interfere with pheromone release and dispersal.

items.text: However, rainfall does not eliminate the pest population because eggs, larvae, and pupae occur in protected sites.
Sensitivity to Insecticides
The adult insect is exposed to insecticides mainly through:

items: Exposure to insecticide residues on leaves.

items.text: Moths may die after treatment, but targeting adults alone is insufficient because the field generally contains:
A female can lay a large proportion of its eggs during the first days of its life. Therefore, it may lay eggs before dying if insecticidal action is delayed.
The efficacy of insecticides against moths is affected by:

items: Repeated use of the same IRAC group.

items.text: Natural Enemies of the Adult Insect
Adult moths are preyed upon by generalist predators such as:

items: Organisms feeding on moths near the soil surface.

items.text: Adult insects may also become infected with some entomopathogenic fungi when humidity and contact are sufficient.
However, the adult stage is mobile and capable of flight, so its exposure to natural enemies differs from the relatively stationary egg and larval stages.
Distinguishing the Adult from Similar Moths
Color and size alone cannot be used to identify Tuta absoluta because it is externally similar to other small moths.
Species or groups with which it may be confused include:

items: Other species in the family Gelechiidae.

items.text: Difference from Potato Tuber Moth
Both species:

items: Belong to the genus Phthorimaea under modern classification.
Are associated with the family Solanaceae.

items.text: Scale and spot patterns may differ, but reliable diagnosis depends on:
Difference from Tomato Pinworm
The two insects are similar in:
Therefore, separation requires diagnostic expertise and examination of the genitalia.
Pheromone Traps
Pheromone traps simulate the sexual odor released by the female.
The trap primarily attracts:
Male Tuta absoluta.
They are used for:

items: Determining the beginning of moth activity.
Identifying periods of increase and decline.
Mass trapping when used at suitable densities.

items.text: However, the number of captured males does not equal:
Trap catches may be low while larvae remain inside plants, or high because males are immigrating from a neighboring field.
Therefore, traps must be interpreted together with:
Mating Disruption
Mating disruption depends on releasing a quantity of synthetic pheromone throughout the production area so that the air becomes saturated with multiple chemical signals.
This makes it difficult for:
This method performs better when:

items: The greenhouse is well sealed or protected.
Immigration of mated females from outside is prevented.
No severe neighboring outbreaks are present.

items.text: Mating disruption does not kill eggs or larvae already present and must therefore be integrated with other components of integrated pest management.
Mass Trapping
Mass trapping increases trap density in order to remove a large proportion of males.
Reducing male numbers decreases mating probability, particularly when the initial pest density is low.
Its efficacy declines when:
Traps are not a substitute for residue removal, plant inspection, biological control, and selective chemical control.
Collection and Preservation of Moths
Adult insects are collected from:
When collecting a specimen for diagnosis:

items: 1. Do not hold the moth by the wings.
2. Use a fine brush or soft forceps.
3. Keep the specimen away from moisture.
4. Prevent several moths from rubbing against one another.
5. Record trap data, location, and date.
7. Preserve the specimen according to morphological or molecular examination requirements.

items.text: Moths in sticky traps may be damaged by:

items: Body decomposition when traps are left for long periods.

items.text: An adult reared from a larva or pupa inside a clean container is preferable when genital examination is required.
Field Examination of the Moth
External morphology is examined with a hand lens or stereomicroscope while recording:

items: Number and distribution of dark spots.

items.text: A match with a single characteristic is not sufficient to confirm the species, especially in areas where related solanaceous moths occur.
Applied Importance of the Adult Insect
The importance of the moth in integrated pest management is represented by the following:
Early Warning
Males appear in traps before severe damage is observed, allowing inspection intensity to increase.
Identifying the Source of Immigration
A sudden increase at field edges may reveal movement of moths from a nearby old field.
Predicting Egg Laying
Increased male activity in the presence of females indicates the possibility of a new wave of eggs and larvae.
Evaluating Agricultural Sanitation
Continued trap catches after harvest indicate continued moth emergence from pupae or immigration from residues and neighboring fields.
Mating Disruption
The moth is the direct target of pheromone dispensers.
Mass Trapping
Traps reduce male numbers when used at suitable densities.
Preventing Spread
Cleaning packages, vehicles, and packing stations helps prevent movement of moths and pupae into new areas.
Common Errors in Interpreting the Adult Stage
Error One: The Moth Is the Stage That Bores into the Fruit
The larva mines plant tissues, whereas the moth lays eggs.
Error Two: Failure to See Moths Means There Is No Infestation
Moths hide during the day, and larvae may remain inside the plant even when moth activity is low.
Error Three: Every Small Gray Moth Is Tuta
Similar species occur, and the moth must be linked to damage, pheromone response, and diagnostic examination.
Error Four: Trap Catch Equals the Number of Insects per Feddan
The trap measures male activity around it and is affected by location, wind, lure performance, and plant density.
Error Five: Low Trap Catch Means Complete Control Success
Male catches may decline while eggs, larvae, and pupae remain in the field.
Error Six: Traps Attract Mated Females
Conventional sex-pheromone traps primarily attract males and do not remove females present in the field.
Error Seven: Replacing the Lure Alone Is Sufficient When the Trap Is Full
The trap also requires cleaning or replacement of the adhesive surface and adjustment of height and location.

items.text: Summary of the Adult Insect
The adult of Tuta absoluta is a small moth whose body length is usually approximately 6–7 mm and whose forewing length ranges from 4.2 to 5.0 mm.
Its coloration is silvery gray or grayish brown, with dark and pale scales and small black spots on the forewings. The hindwings are lighter and bear long marginal fringes.
The moth has long filiform antennae, upturned labial palpi, and three pairs of slender legs. It folds the wings over the body at rest, giving it a long, narrow form that assists camouflage.

Females are larger and fuller than males on average, but accurate sex determination cannot be made from size alone. Confirmatory diagnosis of sex and species depends on the abdominal tip, genitalia, and microscopic or molecular examination.
Moths are most active during evening and night and hide during the day within foliage, crop residues, and shaded sites.
The female releases a sex pheromone that attracts males. This is the scientific basis of pheromone traps, mating disruption, and mass trapping.
The female lays the largest proportion of its eggs during the first days after mating, and its lifetime egg production may range from tens of eggs to more than 200 depending on conditions.
The moth causes no direct damage to the plant, but its economic importance is substantial because it moves infestation among plants and fields and produces the next generation of mining larvae.
The integrated pest-management program therefore targets the adult stage through:

items: Prevention of moth migration from old fields.
Linking trap catches to inspection of eggs, larvae, and fruits.
